news_summary: Today's technology news highlights groundbreaking advances in energy-efficient AI, ultra-thin light manipulation, and the discovery of antiviral proteins with potential for powerful molecular applications.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées révolutionnaires dans le domaine de l'IA économe en énergie, de la manipulation de la lumière à l'échelle nanométrique, ainsi que la découverte de protéines antivirales offrant un potentiel pour de puissantes applications moléculaires.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances revolucionarios en inteligencia artificial de bajo consumo, manipulación de la luz en dispositivos ultradelgados y el descubrimiento de proteínas antivirales con gran potencial para aplicaciones moleculares.

articles.title: AI breakthrough cuts energy use by 100x while boosting accuracy
articles.summary: AI is consuming staggering amounts of energy—already over 10% of U.S. electricity—and the demand is only accelerating. Now, researchers have unveiled a radically more efficient approach that could slash AI energy use by up to 100× while actually improving accuracy. By combining neural networks with human-like symbolic reasoning, their system helps robots think more logically instead of relying on brute-force trial and error.
articles.content:
Artificial intelligence is consuming enormous amounts of electricity in the United States. According to the International Energy Agency, AI systems and data centers used about 415 terawatt hours of power in 2024. That accounts for more than 10% of the country's total electricity production, and demand is projected to double by 2030.

This rapid growth has raised concerns about sustainability. In response, researchers at a School of Engineering have created a proof-of-concept AI system designed to be far more efficient. Their approach could reduce energy use by up to 100 times while also improving performance on tasks.

A Hybrid Approach Called Neuro-Symbolic AI

The research comes from the laboratory of Matthias Scheutz, Karol Family Applied Technology Professor. His team is developing neuro-symbolic AI, which combines traditional neural networks with symbolic reasoning. This method mirrors how people approach problems by breaking them into steps and categories.

The work will be presented at the International Conference of Robotics and Automation in Vienna in May and will appear in the conference proceedings.

Teaching Robots to See, Understand, and Act

Unlike familiar large language models (LLMs) such as ChatGPT and Gemini, the team focuses on AI systems used in robotics. These systems are known as visual-language-action (VLA) models. They extend LLM capabilities by incorporating vision and physical movement.

VLA models take in visual data from cameras and instructions from language, then translate that information into real-world actions. For example, they can control a robot's wheels, arms, or fingers to complete a task.

Why Traditional AI Struggles With Simple Tasks

Conventional VLA systems rely heavily on data and trial-and-error learning. If a robot is asked to stack blocks into a tower, it must first analyze the scene, identify each block, and determine how to place them correctly.

This process often leads to mistakes. Shadows may confuse the system about a block's shape, or the robot may place pieces incorrectly, causing the structure to collapse.

These errors are similar to the problems seen in LLMs. Just as robots can misplace blocks, chatbots can generate false or misleading outputs. Examples include fabricating legal cases or producing images with unrealistic details such as extra fingers.

How Symbolic Reasoning Improves Accuracy and Efficiency

Symbolic reasoning offers a different strategy. Instead of relying only on patterns from data, it uses rules and abstract concepts such as shape and balance. This allows the system to plan more effectively and avoid unnecessary trial and error.

"Like an LLM, VLA models act on statistical results from large training sets of similar scenarios, but that can lead to errors," said Scheutz. "A neuro-symbolic VLA can apply rules that limit the amount of trial and error during learning and get to a solution much faster. Not only does it complete the task much faster, but the time spent on training the system is significantly reduced."

Strong Results in Puzzle Tests

The researchers tested their system using the Tower of Hanoi puzzle, a classic problem that requires careful planning.

The neuro-symbolic VLA achieved a 95% success rate, compared with just 34% for standard systems. When given a more complex version of the puzzle that it had not encountered before, the hybrid system still succeeded 78% of the time. Traditional models failed every attempt.

Training time also dropped sharply. The new system learned the task in only 34 minutes, while conventional models required more than a day and a half.

Massive Energy Savings in Training and Use

Energy consumption was reduced dramatically as well. Training the neuro-symbolic model required only 1% of the energy used by a standard VLA system. During operation, it used just 5% of the energy needed by conventional approaches.

Scheutz compared this inefficiency to everyday AI tools. "These systems are just trying to predict the next word or action in a sequence, but that can be imperfect, and they can come up with inaccurate results or hallucinations. Their energy expense is often disproportionate to the task. For example, when you search on Google, the AI summary at the top of the page consumes up to 100 times more energy than the generation of the website listings."

The Growing Strain of AI on Power Infrastructure

As AI adoption accelerates across industries, demand for computing power continues to climb. Companies are building increasingly large data centers, some of which require hundreds of megawatts of electricity. That level of consumption can exceed the needs of entire small cities.

This trend has sparked a race to expand infrastructure, raising concerns about long-term energy limits.

A More Sustainable Path for AI

The researchers suggest that current approaches based on LLMs and VLAs may not be sustainable in the long run. While these systems are powerful, they consume large amounts of energy and can still produce unreliable results.

In contrast, neuro-symbolic AI offers a different direction. By combining learning with structured reasoning, it may provide a more efficient and dependable foundation for future AI systems.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in AI technology that drastically reduces energy consumption by up to 100 times while improving accuracy. This innovation addresses the growing environmental and sustainability concerns related to AI's massive electricity use, offering a scalable, impactful solution with broad implications for the future of AI systems and energy infrastructure.
articles.personality_title: New AI method cuts energy use by 100 times while improving accuracy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial intelligence (AI) is using a huge amount of electricity in the United States, more than 10% of all power in 2024. This high energy use is expected to double by 2030, raising concerns about how sustainable AI is as it grows.

**What happened** – Researchers led by Matthias Scheutz developed a new AI system combining neural networks with symbolic reasoning, called neuro-symbolic AI. This system helps robots think more like humans by using rules and logic rather than just trial and error. They tested it on a puzzle called the Tower of Hanoi and found it was much faster, more accurate, and used far less energy than traditional AI.

**Impact** – This new AI method cut energy use by up to 100 times compared to older systems, while improving success rates from 34% to 95% on simple tasks. It also learned tasks much faster, reducing training time from over a day to just 34 minutes. This means AI can become more reliable and less costly to run, which is important as AI systems grow and use more power worldwide.

**What's next step** – The researchers will present their work at a major robotics conference in Vienna. The hope is that this neuro-symbolic approach can be developed further and applied to many types of AI, especially robots that need to see, understand, and act in the real world. This could lead to more efficient AI tools that use less electricity and make fewer mistakes.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new AI approach combining brain-like reasoning with learning can make robots smarter and use 100 times less energy than current AI systems.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelle méthode d'IA réduit la consommation d'énergie par 100 tout en améliorant la précision
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'intelligence artificielle (IA) consomme une énorme quantité d'électricité aux États-Unis, plus de 10 % de toute l'énergie en 2024. Cette consommation élevée devrait doubler d'ici 2030, ce qui suscite des inquiétudes sur la durabilité de l'IA.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs dirigés par Matthias Scheutz ont développé un nouveau système d'IA combinant réseaux neuronaux et raisonnement symbolique, appelé IA neuro-symbolique. Ce système aide les robots à penser plus comme des humains en utilisant des règles et de la logique plutôt que des essais et erreurs. Ils l'ont testé sur un puzzle appelé la Tour de Hanoï et ont constaté qu'il était beaucoup plus rapide, précis et consommait beaucoup moins d'énergie que les IA traditionnelles.

**Impact** – Cette nouvelle méthode d'IA a réduit la consommation d'énergie jusqu'à 100 fois par rapport aux anciens systèmes, tout en améliorant le taux de réussite de 34 % à 95 % sur des tâches simples. Elle a aussi appris les tâches beaucoup plus rapidement, réduisant le temps d'entraînement de plus d'un jour à seulement 34 minutes. Cela signifie que l'IA peut devenir plus fiable et moins coûteuse à utiliser, ce qui est important alors que les systèmes d'IA grandissent et consomment plus d'énergie dans le monde.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs présenteront leurs travaux lors d'une grande conférence de robotique à Vienne. L'espoir est que cette approche neuro-symbolique puisse être développée davantage et appliquée à de nombreux types d'IA, en particulier les robots qui doivent voir, comprendre et agir dans le monde réel. Cela pourrait conduire à des outils d'IA plus efficaces qui consomment moins d'électricité et font moins d'erreurs.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle approche d'IA combinant raisonnement humain et apprentissage peut rendre les robots plus intelligents et utiliser 100 fois moins d'énergie que les systèmes actuels.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método de IA reduce el consumo de energía 100 veces y mejora la precisión
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inteligencia artificial (IA) está usando una gran cantidad de electricidad en Estados Unidos, más del 10 % de toda la energía en 2024. Se espera que este consumo aumente al doble para 2030, lo que preocupa sobre la sostenibilidad de la IA.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores dirigidos por Matthias Scheutz crearon un nuevo sistema de IA que combina redes neuronales con razonamiento simbólico, llamado IA neuro-simbólica. Este sistema ayuda a los robots a pensar más como humanos usando reglas y lógica en lugar de solo prueba y error. Lo probaron con un rompecabezas llamado Torre de Hanoi y descubrieron que era mucho más rápido, preciso y consumía mucha menos energía que los sistemas de IA tradicionales.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo método de IA redujo el consumo de energía hasta 100 veces en comparación con los sistemas anteriores, mientras mejoraba la tasa de éxito del 34 % al 95 % en tareas simples. También aprendió las tareas mucho más rápido, reduciendo el tiempo de entrenamiento de más de un día a solo 34 minutos. Esto significa que la IA puede ser más confiable y menos costosa de usar, algo importante a medida que los sistemas de IA crecen y consumen más energía en todo el mundo.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores presentarán su trabajo en una importante conferencia de robótica en Viena. Esperan que este enfoque neuro-simbólico pueda desarrollarse más y aplicarse a muchos tipos de IA, especialmente robots que necesitan ver, entender y actuar en el mundo real. Esto podría llevar a herramientas de IA más eficientes que usen menos electricidad y cometan menos errores.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo enfoque de IA que combina razonamiento humano con aprendizaje puede hacer que los robots sean más inteligentes y usen 100 veces menos energía que los sistemas actuales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing, intricately balanced tower of interlocking blocks floating above a softly lit circuit board with subtle neural network patterns, surrounded by gentle beams of light symbolizing energy efficiency and clarity, rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft blues.

articles.title: Scientists trap light in a layer 1,000x thinner than hair
articles.summary: Researchers have created a nanoscale structure that traps infrared light in a layer just 40 nanometers thick—over 1,000 times thinner than a human hair. By using a unique material with exceptional light-bending properties, they can confine and intensify light far beyond previous limits. This setup also dramatically boosts light conversion effects, turning infrared into visible blue light. The advance could pave the way for smaller, faster photonic technologies.
articles.content:
Researchers from the Faculty of Physics at the University of Warsaw, working with teams from the Łódź University of Technology, the Warsaw University of Technology, and the Polish Academy of Sciences, have created a structure capable of trapping infrared light in a layer just 40 nanometers thick. Their approach relies on a design known as a subwavelength grating made from a specialized material called molybdenum diselenide (MoSe 2 ). The findings were reported in the journal ACS Nano.

Manipulating light at extremely small scales is key to advancing modern technology. As traditional electronics begin to reach their limits, photonics offers an alternative by using light instead of electrons to carry information. Because photons move faster and do not have mass like electrons, devices based on light could become both quicker and smaller, opening the door to more powerful and compact technologies.

The Challenge of Light's Wavelength

Light behaves both as a particle and as a wave, and this wave nature introduces a limitation. Each type of light has a wavelength, which determines how small a structure can be while still controlling it effectively. Visible light has wavelengths of several hundred nanometers, while infrared light extends to a micrometer or more. This raises an important question: can light be confined in structures smaller than its own wavelength?

The research team demonstrated that this is indeed possible. By engineering a subwavelength grating, they were able to trap infrared light within a layer only 40 nanometers thick. This structure consists of closely spaced parallel strips that interact with light similarly to a prism. When these strips are positioned closer together than the wavelength of light, the grating can act like a near-perfect mirror while also holding the light inside a very small volume.

Why Molybdenum Diselenide Works So Well

Earlier versions of such gratings, made from materials like silicon or gallium compounds, required thicknesses of several hundred nanometers to function effectively. Reducing their size caused them to lose their ability to confine light. The key difference in this new approach is the use of molybdenum diselenide, which has a much higher refractive index. In simple terms, light slows down more inside this material than in others. While light slows by about 1.5 times in glass and roughly 3.5 times in silicon or gallium arsenide, it slows by about 4.5 times in MoSe 2 . This strong slowing effect allows the structure to shrink dramatically while still trapping light efficiently, resulting in a layer more than a thousand times thinner than a human hair.

Turning Infrared Light Into Blue Light

MoSe 2 also brings additional advantages. Like graphene, it forms layered structures, but unlike graphene, it is a semiconductor. It also exhibits nonlinear optical behavior, including a process known as third harmonic generation. In this process, three infrared photons combine into one photon with a higher frequency, effectively converting infrared light into visible blue light. Because the grating strongly concentrates infrared light, this conversion becomes much more efficient. The researchers found that the effect is more than 1,500 times stronger compared to a flat layer of the same material.

Another major advance lies in how the material was produced. Previously, thin layers of MoSe 2 were created using exfoliation -- a method similar to peeling layers off a crystal with adhesive tape. While simple, this technique is inconsistent and limited to very small areas, typically around ten square micrometers, which is not suitable for real-world devices.

To overcome this, the team used molecular beam epitaxy (MBE), a well-established method for growing semiconductor layers. This approach allowed them to produce large, uniform MoSe 2 films spanning several square inches. Despite this large size, the layer maintained a thickness of just 40 nanometers, giving it an extreme aspect ratio. For comparison, the thickness-to-size ratio of this layer is about one to a million, while a typical A4 sheet of paper has a ratio closer to 1:2000.

Toward Practical Photonic Applications

These results suggest that molybdenum diselenide produced in this way could significantly change how light is controlled in future technologies. Structures no longer need to be thick to manipulate light effectively. Instead, extremely thin layers can perform the same function, and in some cases even better. Because the production method is scalable, the path toward real-world applications, such as photonic integrated circuits, is becoming increasingly realistic.

Funding and Support

The research was funded by the National Science Centre under projects OPUS 2020/39/B/ST7/03502 and 2021/41/B/ST3/04183, with European Union funds under ERC-ADVANCED grant No. 101053716, the Foundation for Polish Science under project ENG.02.01-IP.05-T004/23, and by the University of Warsaw under the Excellence Initiative - Research University (IDUB) New Ideas in Priority Research Areas II No. 501-D111-20-2004310 titled "Ultrathin subwavelength gratings based on dichalcogenides."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in photonics where researchers have created an ultrathin nanoscale structure that traps infrared light in a layer over 1,000 times thinner than a human hair. This advance has broad implications for developing smaller, faster photonic technologies that could surpass traditional electronics, benefiting society by enabling more powerful and compact devices. The article provides detailed context on the materials, methods, and potential applications, demonstrating substantial substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: Scientists trap infrared light in a layer 1,000 times thinner than a hair
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are working to control light in very small spaces to build faster and smaller technologies that use light instead of electricity. This field is called photonics, and it could improve devices like computers and sensors.

**What happened** – Researchers from several Polish universities created a tiny structure that can trap infrared light in a layer only 40 nanometers thick. This is more than 1,000 times thinner than a human hair. They used a special material called molybdenum diselenide (MoSe2) to build a pattern that acts like a mirror and holds the light inside.

**Impact** – This is important because light usually needs bigger spaces to be controlled. Using MoSe2, the researchers made a much thinner layer that still traps light very well. The structure also changes infrared light into visible blue light much more efficiently than before. Plus, they found a way to make large, uniform films of this material, which is good for making real devices.

**What's next step** – The team’s method can be used to produce thin layers for practical photonic devices. This could lead to smaller and faster parts for technologies like optical computers or sensors. Future work will focus on integrating these layers into real systems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists developed an ultra-thin material that traps and changes infrared light, paving the way for smaller and more powerful light-based technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques piègent la lumière infrarouge dans une couche 1 000 fois plus fine qu’un cheveu
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à contrôler la lumière dans des espaces très petits pour créer des technologies plus rapides et plus compactes utilisant la lumière au lieu de l’électricité. Ce domaine s’appelle la photonique et pourrait améliorer des appareils comme les ordinateurs et les capteurs.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de plusieurs universités polonaises ont créé une structure minuscule capable de piéger la lumière infrarouge dans une couche de seulement 40 nanomètres d’épaisseur. C’est plus de 1 000 fois plus fin qu’un cheveu humain. Ils ont utilisé un matériau spécial appelé diséléniure de molybdène (MoSe2) pour fabriquer un motif qui agit comme un miroir et retient la lumière.

**Impact** – C’est important car la lumière nécessite normalement des espaces plus grands pour être contrôlée. Grâce au MoSe2, les chercheurs ont fabriqué une couche beaucoup plus fine qui piège la lumière efficacement. La structure transforme aussi la lumière infrarouge en lumière bleue visible avec une efficacité bien meilleure. De plus, ils ont trouvé un moyen de produire de grands films uniformes de ce matériau, ce qui est utile pour fabriquer des appareils réels.

**Prochaine étape** – Leur méthode peut être utilisée pour produire des couches fines destinées à des dispositifs photoniques pratiques. Cela pourrait conduire à des composants plus petits et plus rapides pour des technologies comme les ordinateurs optiques ou les capteurs. Les travaux futurs viseront à intégrer ces couches dans des systèmes réels.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont développé un matériau ultra-fin qui piège et transforme la lumière infrarouge, ouvrant la voie à des technologies optiques plus petites et plus puissantes.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos atrapan luz infrarroja en una capa 1,000 veces más delgada que un cabello
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos trabajan para controlar la luz en espacios muy pequeños y así crear tecnologías más rápidas y compactas que usen luz en lugar de electricidad. Este campo se llama fotónica y podría mejorar dispositivos como computadoras y sensores.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de varias universidades polacas crearon una estructura diminuta que puede atrapar luz infrarroja en una capa de solo 40 nanómetros de grosor. Esto es más de 1,000 veces más delgado que un cabello humano. Usaron un material especial llamado diseleniuro de molibdeno (MoSe2) para construir un patrón que actúa como un espejo y retiene la luz.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque la luz normalmente necesita espacios más grandes para ser controlada. Usando MoSe2, los investigadores hicieron una capa mucho más delgada que atrapa la luz muy bien. La estructura también convierte la luz infrarroja en luz azul visible con mucha más eficiencia. Además, encontraron una forma de fabricar películas grandes y uniformes de este material, lo que es útil para crear dispositivos reales.

**Próximo paso** – El método del equipo se puede usar para producir capas delgadas para dispositivos fotónicos prácticos. Esto podría conducir a partes más pequeñas y rápidas para tecnologías como computadoras ópticas o sensores. El trabajo futuro se centrará en integrar estas capas en sistemas reales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos desarrollaron un material ultrafino que atrapa y transforma la luz infrarroja, abriendo el camino a tecnologías basadas en luz más pequeñas y potentes.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an ultra-thin, glowing layered structure made of delicate parallel strips thinner than a human hair, emitting soft blue light as waves of infrared light gently flow into and become trapped within it, set against a simple, muted background that emphasizes the contrast between the microscopic scale and the vastness of light’s transformation.

articles.title: ‘Treasure trove’ of antiviral proteins could inspire powerful molecular tools
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 02 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01011-yTwo research teams mined genomic data from bacteria to create databases containing thousands of antiviral defence proteins that could inspire powerful biotechnologies.
articles.content:
Escherichia coli produce a diverse array of proteins to help protect them against bacteriophages (green). Credit: M. Maeder/Dept of Microbiology, Biozentrum/SPL

Bacteria have been fighting off viruses using a huge arsenal of molecular weaponry that scientists did not know about — until now.

In studies published in Science today, two research teams describe the machine-learning algorithms they developed to screen bacterial genomes and identify proteins that are involved in protecting the microorganisms against viral invaders. Their analyses identified hundreds of thousands of potential antiviral proteins, which researchers could harness to develop innovative biotechnologies.

“This is a treasure trove for any biochemist,” says José Antonio Escudero, a microbiologist at the CSIC National Center for Biotechnology in Madrid, who was not involved in either study.

Previous discoveries of antiviral immune systems in bacteria include the gene-editing system CRISPR–Cas9 and DNA-snipping proteins called restriction enzymes. Researchers have repurposed both systems to create molecular tools for genetic engineering.

“There’s a hope that maybe there’s a next generation of molecular tools that would come from some of these new systems,” says Michael Laub, a microbiologist at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in Cambridge and a co-author on one of the studies1.

Protein defences

When looking at the genome of a bacterium, “most of it is still dark matter”, says Escudero. “There are many things that we don’t know how they work or what they are.”

Previous studies had confirmed that bacteria use more than 250 proteins to protect themselves from viral infections. Researchers had suggested that the true bacterial immune system was much larger and more diverse than this, “But the big question was how much diversity and how can we actually predict it at scale?”, says Aude Bernheim, a microbiologist at the Pasteur Institute in Paris and a co-author on one of the papers2.

Bacteria use a decoy defence molecule to set a trap for viruses

In their study2, Bernheim and her colleagues trained deep-learning models on protein and genomic data to predict antiviral systems. Their goal was to identify the “full breadth of diversity of bacterial immunity”, says Bernheim. The analysis estimated that, on average, 1.5% of genes in a bacterial genome correspond to proteins that are involved in antiviral immunity — three times more than previous estimates. And more than 85% of the predicted protein families were not previously associated with immunity. Laboratory experiments on two bacterial species — Escherichia coli and Streptomyces albus — confirmed the presence of 12 ‘antiphage’ systems that ward off viruses called bacteriophages that infect bacteria. The systems had not previously been linked to antiviral defence.

In their paper, Laub and his colleagues1 designed a separate machine-learning tool called DefensePredictor, which predicts bacterial immune proteins on the basis of gene and protein data from 17,000 bacterial genomes.

When tested on 69 diverse strains of E. coli, DefensePredictor identified 624 proteins as defence-related immune systems, more than 100 of which were previously unknown. In lab experiments, Laub and his colleagues confirmed the defence activity in 42 cases.

Both studies come to the same conclusion: that researchers have been “massively underestimating the number of defence systems”, says Laub. The research “brings to light how many systems are out there still to be characterized”, he adds.

The discoveries include “hundreds of genes that we didn’t know were related”, says Escudero.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers used machine learning to uncover a vast number of previously unknown bacterial antiviral proteins. These discoveries have broad implications for developing new molecular tools, potentially revolutionizing biotechnology and genetic engineering, which can benefit society at large. The article provides detailed context about the studies, their methods, and confirmed experimental results, demonstrating substantial substance and significance.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover thousands of new bacterial antiviral proteins using AI
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Bacteria have natural ways to protect themselves from viruses. Scientists have known about some of these proteins, like CRISPR, which have helped us create powerful tools for genetic research. But many bacterial proteins and their functions remained unknown.

**What happened** – Two research teams used artificial intelligence (AI) to study the genes of thousands of bacteria. They found hundreds of thousands of proteins that might help bacteria defend against viruses. Their computers looked at patterns in bacterial DNA and predicted which proteins are involved in fighting viruses. They tested some of these proteins in the lab and confirmed many are real antiviral defenders.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows there are far more bacterial defense proteins than scientists thought—about three times more. Many of these proteins were never linked to immunity before. Finding so many new proteins means scientists have a huge new source of tools to study and use. These proteins could inspire new technologies for medicine, agriculture, and biotechnology.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to study these proteins to understand exactly how they work. They hope to develop new molecular tools based on these proteins, similar to how CRISPR changed genetic engineering. This could lead to better ways to fight diseases or improve crops in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using AI, scientists uncovered thousands of previously unknown bacterial proteins that protect against viruses, opening doors for new biotechnologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des milliers de nouvelles protéines antivirales bactériennes grâce à l’IA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les bactéries possèdent des moyens naturels pour se protéger contre les virus. Les scientifiques connaissaient certaines de ces protéines, comme CRISPR, qui ont aidé à créer des outils puissants pour la recherche génétique. Mais beaucoup de protéines bactériennes et leurs fonctions restaient inconnues.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Deux équipes de recherche ont utilisé l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour étudier les gènes de milliers de bactéries. Elles ont trouvé des centaines de milliers de protéines qui pourraient aider les bactéries à se défendre contre les virus. Leurs ordinateurs ont analysé des motifs dans l’ADN bactérien et prédit quelles protéines sont impliquées dans la lutte contre les virus. Elles ont testé certaines de ces protéines en laboratoire et confirmé que beaucoup sont de vrais défenseurs antiviraux.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre qu’il y a bien plus de protéines de défense bactérienne que ce que les scientifiques pensaient – environ trois fois plus. Beaucoup de ces protéines n’étaient jamais associées à l’immunité auparavant. Trouver autant de nouvelles protéines signifie que les scientifiques ont une énorme nouvelle source d’outils à étudier et à utiliser. Ces protéines pourraient inspirer de nouvelles technologies pour la médecine, l’agriculture et la biotechnologie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront d’étudier ces protéines pour comprendre exactement comment elles fonctionnent. Ils espèrent développer de nouveaux outils moléculaires basés sur ces protéines, comme CRISPR a transformé l’ingénierie génétique. Cela pourrait mener à de meilleures façons de combattre les maladies ou d’améliorer les cultures à l’avenir.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Grâce à l’IA, des scientifiques ont découvert des milliers de protéines bactériennes antivirales inconnues, ouvrant la voie à de nouvelles biotechnologies.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren miles de nuevas proteínas antivirales bacterianas usando IA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las bacterias tienen formas naturales de protegerse contra virus. Los científicos conocían algunas de estas proteínas, como CRISPR, que han ayudado a crear herramientas poderosas para la investigación genética. Pero muchas proteínas bacterianas y sus funciones seguían siendo desconocidas.

**Qué pasó** – Dos equipos de investigación usaron inteligencia artificial (IA) para estudiar los genes de miles de bacterias. Encontraron cientos de miles de proteínas que podrían ayudar a las bacterias a defenderse de los virus. Sus computadoras analizaron patrones en el ADN bacteriano y predijeron qué proteínas están involucradas en la defensa contra virus. Probaron algunas en el laboratorio y confirmaron que muchas son verdaderos defensores antivirales.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que hay muchas más proteínas de defensa bacteriana de lo que se pensaba, aproximadamente tres veces más. Muchas de estas proteínas nunca se habían relacionado con la inmunidad antes. Encontrar tantas proteínas nuevas significa que los científicos tienen una gran fuente de herramientas nuevas para estudiar y usar. Estas proteínas podrían inspirar nuevas tecnologías para la medicina, la agricultura y la biotecnología.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán estudiando estas proteínas para entender exactamente cómo funcionan. Esperan desarrollar nuevas herramientas moleculares basadas en estas proteínas, como CRISPR cambió la ingeniería genética. Esto podría llevar a mejores formas de combatir enfermedades o mejorar cultivos en el futuro.

**Resumen en una frase** – Usando IA, científicos descubrieron miles de proteínas bacterianas antivirales desconocidas, abriendo puertas a nuevas biotecnologías.
